This supports my agreement with \boxed{B} - minocycline's association with flagellate pigmentation over other listed options. Based on the retrieved evidence, I reaffirm my stance that minocycline is the drug causing flagellate pigmentation. Specifically, the evidence confirms that minocycline leads to characteristic iron-containing compound accumulations within dermal macrophages, resulting in the formation of black-blue lines reminiscent of "whip-like" depictions often referred to as "flagellation." While the text mentions other drugs capable of inducing various forms of pigmentation, none fit the exact description provided better than minocycline. Given the detailed explanations linking minocycline directly to this phenomenon, coupled with the need to exclude other potential culprits rigorously through patient history and clinical evaluations, I confidently stand by my earlier choice. Hence, confirming again \boxed{B}. Minocycline emerges as distinctly aligned with documented cases showing this specific type of skin change compared to alternatives evaluated.
